design_philosophy.core_loop: Select family member → explore dungeon → defeat monsters for resources → solve character-specific puzzles → acquire crowns → return home to switch characters → repeat until all crowns collected → face final boss
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Character capability constraints vs. environmental puzzles; each family member has unique strengths offset by proportionate weaknesses, requiring strategic character selection and resource management
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Lead a multi-generational family quest through an expansive underground labyrinth, leveraging each member's special abilities to overcome magical obstacles and ultimately slay an ancient dragon
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Rigid character specialization philosophy: the game mandates using all family members at some point by gating content behind specific abilities (e.g., mattock for breaking blocks, wings for flight, gloves for moving special blocks). This is uncompromising—no workarounds exist. Also philosophically opinionated about non-linearity: five major dungeon sections can be tackled in various orders, predating Metroidvania conventions.

difficulty.continues_system: Password system allows resuming progress without restarting from the beginning; player must periodically return to family household to obtain passwords
difficulty.checkpointing: Soft checkpointing via password mechanic tied to returning home; no mid-dungeon save points documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency through character selection strategy and resource management (money, health potions, magic potions). Player chooses which dungeon section to tackle and when, based on which character abilities they have available. Combat uses magic power as a resource cost for attacks, creating meaningful consumption decisions.

progression.upgrade_system: Magical items acquired from chests or purchased in shops provide temporary or persistent power-ups (health/magic restoration, attack boost, range extension, environmental effects like flight or block manipulation). Items have character-specific restrictions. Money collected from defeated monsters serves as upgrade currency.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Character-gated progression: each dungeon section is designed with one character in mind and requires that character's unique ability to access. Four crowns must be collected sequentially (or in player-chosen order across five sections). Final boss only accessible after all crowns acquired and used by Roas to manifest the Dragon Slayer sword.
replayability_hooks: Multiple character playstyles encourage replaying with different family members
Non-linear dungeon structure allows different completion routes
Secret items and shops in impractical locations reward exploration

world_building_techniques: Family household serves as narrative and mechanical hub, grounding the story in domestic unity
Character introductions via select screen and credits establish roles (Warrior, Wizard, Ranger, Elf, Pet)
Instruction manual contains nearly all plot exposition (Drasle family backstory, Keela the ancient dragon, four crowns quest, Dragon Slayer sword mythology)
Environmental design: five distinct dungeon sections with different music and visual characteristics reinforce world geography
Shops and inns scattered throughout dungeon suggest civilization and commerce existing within the labyrinth

boss_design.philosophy: Four intermediate bosses (one per crown-guarding section) plus final boss (Keela). Boss encounters appear designed to test character-specific abilities and reward player mastery of that character's moveset. Limited pattern documentation in sources.
notable_bosses: Keela (King Dragon/ancient dragon, final boss, trapped in painting within underground labyrinth, only defeatable with Dragon Slayer sword)

faction_indicators: Underground civilization represented by shops and inns within dungeon
Ancient dragon Keela as opposing force trapped in magical prison
danger_signals: Endless monster supply throughout dungeon sections
Poison items dropped by some monsters inflict harm
Traps and puzzles present throughout convoluted passageways
Special blocks and environmental obstacles block progression until correct character obtained
Keela's magical imprisonment suggests powerful ancient threat requiring family unity to defeat
environmental_storytelling: Family portrait of Jiela's great-great-grandfather Lark on household wall establishes lineage depth
Five distinctly characterized dungeon sections (visual style, music, design philosophy for specific character) create sense of expansive, purposefully-designed underworld
Household structure as safe zone contrasts with dangerous labyrinth, reinforcing stakes
Shop and inn placement in impractical locations suggests dungeon has organic history beyond player's quest

sources_used: tcrf: Legacy of the Wizard - The Cutting Room Floor (unused graphics, music, sound test, regional differences, character sprites)
wikipedia: Legacy of the Wizard (plot, gameplay mechanics, character abilities, dungeon structure, item system, password system)
